coredata.dc:title: Spontaneous formation of carbon dots helps to distinguish molecular fluorophores species
coredata.dc:creator: Gómez, I. Jénnifer
Sulleiro, Manuel Vázquez
Pizúrová, Naděžda
Bednařík, Antonín
Lepcio, Petr
Holec, David
Preisler, Jan
Zajíčková, Lenka
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) display photophysical features that appear similar, if not identical, despite the variety of synthesis protocols used to obtain them. The unique design of CDs toward the desired properties requires an exhaustive understanding of the structure itself. This work offers a fundamental insight into the structure and formation of the carbon dots that allows distinguishing the existence of molecular fluorophore species. Remarkably, density functional theory calculations of the elucidated structures support the spontaneous formation of a compact network of molecular species. In addition, as a proof-of-principle, our work comprehensively studies the photoluminescence properties (i.e., concentration, pH and solvent dependencies), which pave the way for developing CD-based logic gate fluorescent sensors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon fluorescent nanoparticles
Carbon dots
Molecular species
Photoluminescence
DFT

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Carbon dots spontaneous formation – Size and morphology
Concentration, pH and solvent dependent photoluminescence

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Ministry of Education, Youth and Sports of the Czech Republic

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministerio de Asuntos Economicos y Transformacion Digital

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by Operational Program Research, Development and Education-Project “MSCAfellow4@MUNI” (CZ.02.2.69/0.0/0.0/20_079/0017045). The authors acknowledge CzechNanoLab Research Infrastructure (LM2018110) supported by the Ministry of Education, Youth and Sports of the Czech Republic (MEYS CR). The authors are grateful to Prof. Emilio Pérez, Prof. Petr Klán, and Prof. Vladimír Šindelář for allowing to use of the TGA, UV–Vis and fluorescence spectrometer, and the MW reactor. Financial resources related to these equipment were RECETOX research infrastructure (supported by MEYS CR under LM2018121 and 02.1.01/0.0/0.0/18_046/0015975) and the CETOCOEN EXCELLENCE Teaming 2 project Horizon2020 (857560). IMDEA Nanociencia acknowledges support from the “Severo Ochoa” Programme for Centres of Excellence in R&D (MINECO, Grant SEV- 2016-0686). The computational results presented have been achieved [in part] using the Vienna Scientific Cluster (VSC).

This work was supported by Operational Program Research, Development and Education-Project “MSCAfellow4@MUNI” ( CZ.02.2.69/0.0/0.0/20_079/0017045 ). The authors acknowledge CzechNanoLab Research Infrastructure ( LM2018110 ) supported by the Ministry of Education, Youth and Sports of the Czech Republic ( MEYS CR ). The authors are grateful to Prof. Emilio Pérez, Prof. Petr Klán, and Prof. Vladimír Šindelář for allowing to use of the TGA, UV–Vis and fluorescence spectrometer, and the MW reactor. Financial resources related to these equipment were RECETOX research infrastructure (supported by MEYS CR under LM2018121 and 02.1.01/0.0/0.0/18_046/0015975 ) and the CETOCOEN EXCELLENCE Teaming 2 project Horizon2020 ( 857560 ). IMDEA Nanociencia acknowledges support from the “Severo Ochoa” Programme for Centres of Excellence in R&D (MINECO, Grant SEV- 2016-0686 ). The computational results presented have been achieved [in part] using the Vienna Scientific Cluster (VSC).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Size and morphology characterization of the CDs: (a), (b), (c) DLS results (photographs of the corresponding powder and water solutions in insets); (d), (e), (f) TEM micrographs. Displayed DLS data correspond to three independent repetitions of the experiment, which is represented by different color intensities. The color-coding is red for 1 week, green for 1 month and blue for 5 months. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Time evolution of the structural and chemical composition of CDs and their comparison with glucose as the starting material. (a) FTIR spectra, (b) XRD patterns, (c) Atomic composition content obtained from XPS measurements. The solid line represents nitrogen, the dashed line oxygen and the dotted line carbon. (d), (e) evolution of the relative amount of C and O and their functional groups in the CDs during the synthesis as obtained from the high resolution XPS measurements. The color-coding is as described in Fig. 1.

Structural characterization of the dots as the function of the time, and reference data for glucose: (a) 1H NMR (inset; zoom between the range 5.7–9 ppm) and (b) DEPT NMR spectra. The color-coding is as described in Fig. 1.

Optical properties of the dots along the time: (a) NUV − Vis absorption specta. PL spectra at different excitation wavelengths at (b) 1 week, (c) 1 month, and (d) 5 months. All the data was recorded in water at room temperature.

PL emission spectrum of CDs excited at: (a) λex = 300 nm for the concentration from 0.015 to 0.25 mg/mL and (b) λex = 340 nm for pH from 2 to 12. Dashed line represented the maximum λem and the solid line fluorescence intensity. The right arrows point the red-shift of emission and the left arrow indicates the blue-shift. The up and down arrows show the increasing and decreasing fluorescence intensity, respectively. λex = 300 and λex 340 nm were chosen as model excitation wavelengths to be more sensitive to changes caused by the concentration and pH, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Synthesis route for the formation of CDs-like structures from glucose and ethylenediamine and its possible representation of the sp2-hybridized aromatic domains. (b) Relative energetic profile obtained by Density Functional Theory of the optimized structures of CDs-like structure.

head.ce:title: Spontaneous formation of carbon dots helps to distinguish molecular fluorophores species

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Condensed Matter Physics, Faculty of Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) display photophysical features that appear similar, if not identical, despite the variety of synthesis protocols used to obtain them. The unique design of CDs toward the desired properties requires an exhaustive understanding of the structure itself. This work offers a fundamental insight into the structure and formation of the carbon dots that allows distinguishing the existence of molecular fluorophore species. Remarkably, density functional theory calculations of the elucidated structures support the spontaneous formation of a compact network of molecular species. In addition, as a proof-of-principle, our work comprehensively studies the photoluminescence properties (i.e., concentration, pH and solvent dependencies), which pave the way for developing CD-based logic gate fluorescent sensors.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), the materials discussed under a variety of terms encompassing carbon nanodots, carbon quantum dots, and graphene quantum dots [1],[2], are a rising star of the carbon nanomaterial family. Their unique properties and promising potential applications have attracted enormous attention since their discovery in 2004 [3–5],[6–11]. In them, favorable attributes of traditional semiconductor-based quantum dots (namely, size- and wavelength-dependent luminescence emission, resistance to photobleaching, ease of bioconjugation) are combined with their fast and cheap synthetic routes and intrinsic non-toxicity [12].

The photoluminescence (PL) mechanism is associated with the carbon core, surface, and molecular fluorophores states and their synergistic effect [13–15]. It remains controversial because of the diversity and complexity of CDs in structure and composition [16]. It is highly dependent on particle size, surface functional groups, graphitization degree, heteroatom doping, etc. [17]. Apart from that, many reports agree that two steps lead to the formation of CDs synthesized via bottom-up methods from small organic molecules: polymerization/condensation and a subsequent carbonization/aromatization (i.e., creation of amorphous or crystalline dots) [18–20]. The synthesis protocols usually require energy to form the CDs. Besides, Rigodanza and co-workers claimed that if the carbonization does not occur, the process yields nanoparticles that are just aggregates of polycyclic aromatic molecules rather than carbon dots [21],[22]. For this reason, it is imperative to seek insight into the structure of the CDs to make a general definition and distinguish them from aggregates of polycyclic aromatic molecules (i.e., molecular species).

The reaction of glucose and ammonia hydroxide supported by microwave energy yielded graphene quantum dots (GQDs) at 220 W [23]. Still, Tang et al. reported a spontaneous formation of nitrogen-doped GQDs (N-GQDs) from the same reactants at room temperature and atmospheric pressure [24]. The resulting structure obtained via microwave hydrothermal synthesis contained many oxygen functional groups (31.9 at.% of oxygen) that remained from the glucose condensation in ammonium solution. At the same time the N/C ratio was relatively low, 0.05, as identified by XPS. The nanoparticles displayed the 0.22 nm d-plane lattice spacing (100) observed by HRTEM and an average height of 3.3 nm according to AFM [23]. The dots obtained spontaneously at room temperature within 8 months had N/C of 0.11, an average height of 4.2 nm, and exhibited the d-plane lattice spacing of 0.21 nm (100) [24]. The similar morphology of the nanoparticles for the two referenced protocols suggests that the process is relatively invariant of delivered energy, although the number of functional groups differs. Also, the optical properties (absorption and fluorescence) were similar, yet not identical. Typical absorption features related to π → π* and n → π* transitions were identified. Concerning the fluorescence, both protocols displayed the fluorescence intensity as a function of excitation and emission wavelengths, with a difference in the position of the maximum fluorescence intensity, which was observed using excitation wavelength of 360 nm (microwave protocol) and 475 nm (room temperature protocol) [23, 24]. Overall, it looks like the differences reside primarily in the as-prepared CDs chemical structure, which leads to the final fluorescence properties.

In this work, we discuss another pair of microwave assisted and spontaneous synthetic protocols. Recently, we have reported a fast microwave-assisted hydrothermal synthesis of nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots from water solution of glucose and ethylenediamine (EDA) [25]. They exhibited tunable fluorescence emission in the entire visible spectral region, which extends to near-ultraviolet and slightly to near-infrared. Now, we have discovered an unexplored spontaneous room temperature synthesis that starts from these two non-conjugated molecules, which undergo onto a nucleophilic addition and followed by water elimination. We investigated the spontaneous carbon dots formation process in a time span of 12 months.

The unknown and unexplored methods to obtain CDs under room temperature could lead to a more detailed description of the formation and structure of the CDs and finally track their relationship with the photophysical properties. It is the lack of clarity in this regard and the unknown link between the structure, formation and properties which hinders the ability of carbon dots for their translation to the real-world applications. Therefore, this work investigates the structure and photophysical properties of a spontaneous CDs-like structure formation and develop a model system to mimic their optical properties. The proposed chemical structures and the theoretical calculations described here provide evidence to the favorable CDs-like structure formation. Subsequently, we explore the PL emission depending on several parameters such as concentration, pH and solvent, which demonstrate their potential as CD-based logic gate fluorescent sensors, which are extensively used in this nano-era [21].

The preparation of the CDs involved a simple bottom-up route. A mixture of glucose (2.5 g, 13.88 mmol) and ethylenediamine (1.3 mL, 19.45 mmol) was dissolved in 100 mL of milli-Q water. The solution was kept at room temperature until a yellowish color was observed (1 week, 1 month, 5 months and 12 months). With increasing, time of growing, the solution changed from colorless to yellow and finally to brownish. The CDs samples were filtered (0.1 μm membranes) to remove the excess unreactive compounds and dialyze against Milli-Q water for 48 h. Afterwards, it was freeze-dried to obtain a yellow or brown powder (Fig. 1 , 33.20 mg of CDs).

DLS was performed in 1 μL quartz cuvettes using DynaPro NanoStar (WYATT, USA). TEM and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were taken with the JEM 2100F transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan). The FTIR spectra were acquired with the Alpha FTIR spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). Raman spectra were recorded with an Alpha300R confocal Raman imaging spectrometer (WITec, Germany) with a 532 nm laser beam. XRD pattern was measured on the SmartLab 3 kW (Rigaku, Japan). The XPS was performed with the Axis Supra spectrometer (Kratos Analytical Ltd., UK). MALDI TOF MS and subAP MALDI MS data were acquired using Autoflex Speed (Bruker, Germany) and Q Exactive Plus Orbitrap (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Germany), respectively. TGA analyses were performed in the Q500 instrument (TA Instruments, France). The absorption spectra were recorded on a Cary 8454 UV–Vis (Agilent Technologies, USA). Fluorescence spectra were measured on a F900 Fluorescent spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments Ltd., UK). Additional details of the above techniques are in Supplementary Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon dots spontaneous formation – Size and morphology
ce:section.ce:para: In Fig. 1, dynamic light scattering (DLS) results and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) micrographs of CDs formed spontaneously after 1 week, 1 month and 5 months are presented. After 1 week of the formation, the DLS showed nanometer-sized particles with an average size of 5.6 ± 1.3 nm (Fig. 1a). Over the time, the hydrodynamic radius average decreased but a few larger particles (below 10 nm) appeared (Fig. 1b,c and Fig. S1). TEM of 1 week sample revealed several types of structures such as carbon-onion type, flake-like structures or ill-shaped particles, apart from amorphous carbon and nanometer-size particles (Fig. 1d Fig. S2). Various interplanar spacings from the last structures were observed ranging between 0.26 and 0.39 nm, which were near to the in-plane lattice constant of graphitic carbon nitride (100) and basal planes spacing (002) of graphite (Fig. S3) [26, 27].

In the case of the sample after 1 month, the carbon dots with crystalline graphite structure were noticed (Fig. 1e). They were significantly smaller compared to the previous sample and representative of carbon dots. Besides, other minority morphologies such as carbon-onion types were observed (Fig. S4). In detail, the crystal nanoparticles presented crystal lattices of 0.20 nm corresponding to the graphite crystal in-plane spacing (Fig. S4) [27]. The sample after 5 months growth represented a clear transition to graphitic nanometer-sized quasispherical particles averaging 3.2 ± 1.3 nm (Fig. 1c,f). It showed crystalline planes with 0.20–0.21 nm separation (Fig. S5), identified as the (100) planes of graphite. Additionally, the nanoparticles stabilized after 5 months in their shape, since almost no differences were observed after 12 months of formation (Fig. S6). Both techniques (TEM and DLS) suggested a decrease in particle size (Fig. S7). We ascribe this behavior to the formation of hydrogen bonds and π-π interactions between their structures. Therefore, the material becomes more compact.

The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of CDs and glucose depicted in Fig. 2a showed similar bands to the already reported CDs [23, 28, 29]. Firstly, new bands were observed between 1596 and 1660 cm−1 which were attributed to CO, CC and CN stretching vibration bands and NH bending bands of amines groups. Moreover, these band were increasing in intensity along the time, suggesting that the number of defects was increasing (Fig. S8). Compared with the glucose, the intensity of the bands lay at around ∼3000–3500 cm−1 and increased due to the new appearance of NH and OH stretching vibrations. The intensity corresponding to the stretching vibrations bands of CC, CO and CN at 1366–1458 cm−1 increased as well along the time. On the contrary, the intensity of the peak at 833 cm−1 (CH) was reduced during the time, indicating that the H content in the dots was lower. The results confirm that the CDs are functionalized with oxygen containing groups such as carboxyl and hydroxyl apart from amine groups.

The Raman spectrum of the CDs displayed two prominent bands centered approximately at 1345 and 1583 cm−1, attributed to the D and G bands (Fig. S9) [25]. The D-to-G band intensity ratio (I
D
/I
G ) was maintained constant at ∼0.96 for all the synthesis time. Considering that the typical I
D
/I
G ratios for carbon-related dots vary between 0.5 and 1.26 [23, 30] this relatively high value indicates the presence of many structural defects.

The XRD patterns in Fig. 2d exhibited a broad diffraction peak around ∼21° and a weak centered at ∼40°, which were attributed to the (002) and (100) lattice spacing of carbon-based materials [31]. No changes are apparent with the increasing time (Fig. S10), suggesting that the carbon nanoparticles where formed already at the early stage. The shifts obtained for our nanoparticles where similar to those fabricated by other approaches [25, 32].

A quantitative elemental analysis was carried out via XPS analysis, including the exact composition of the investigated samples (Table S1-S2). The XPS spectra of the carbon dots samples at different times were compared in Fig. 2c,d,e (further details in SI). The XPS analysis revealed three peaks at around 284 eV, 399 eV, and 532 eV corresponding to the C, N, and O elements in the dots, respectively (Fig. S11). The carbon content remained roughly constant over time; meanwhile, the nitrogen and oxygen content increased and decreased, respectively (Fig. 2c). The high-resolution C 1 s spectrum was composed of four components, which corresponded to the main skeleton of the dots (C–C/C=C), defects of the structures related to heteroatoms (C–O/C–N, C=O/C=N) and finally the COOH group in the edges (Fig. S12–15). The intensity ratio of C–C/C=C and C–O/C–N showed an opposite trend in time (Fig. 2d, Table S1–2). According to how the amount of the C–H/C–C bonds decreases, more defects due to doping were likely created, correlating with the same conclusion observed by FTIR. Additionally, the O1s content on the component CO increased along the time contributing to more defects as well (Fig. 2e, Fig. S12–15). Besides, no differences were observed on the high-resolution of N 1 s. Albeit, their intensity peaks were increasing along the time (Fig. 2c, Fig. S12–15 and Table S1–2). The high-resolution N 1 s spectra did not display any CN bonds, suggesting that most C=O/C=N bonds from the high-resolution C 1 s spectra corresponded mostly to CO. Moreover, the N doping degree was calculated along the times with the N/C ratio and resulted to increase from 0.10 to 0.21 (Table S1).

The 1H NMR spectra of the carbon particles along the time are presented in Fig. 3a and it reveals the presence of four different kinds of chemical species in contrast to glucose which shows only one. The region from 1 to 3 ppm was related with sp3 protons (i.e., CH protons), 3–6 ppm heteroatom protons attached with amines, hydroxyl and ether groups, 6–8 ppm to sp2 or aromatic protons, and 8–9 ppm is identified with the pyrazine ring, aldehydes or carboxyl protons. It is worth to mention that at short times the appearance of signals from 6 to 9 ppm can be barely recognized, but still are present; longer times allowed for more define and intense signals (Fig. S16). On the other hand, the 13C NMR and DEPT spectrum offered an additional proof for the formation of a sp2 graphitic carbon architecture in the dots. Besides we were able to distinguish sp3 carbons from the sp2 ones. As shown in Fig. 3b and Fig. S17, the two main different species were observed between 0 and 80 ppm signals assigned to sp3 carbons and carbons attached with heteroatoms, while the signals between 80 and 200 ppm were attributed to sp2 carbons. Among those peaks assigned to sp2 carbons, the peaks between 80 and 130 ppm were most likely to arise from aromatic carbons, the signals between 160 and 200 ppm are ascribed to the carboxylic and/or carbonyl carbons [33],[34]. Overall, an aromatic network was formed in the carbon particles from the first week of formation as we could observe the CH signals up from the DEPT (80–130 ppm, Fig. 3b and Fig. S17–18). Thereby we conclude that growing the nanoparticles leads to the loss of the aromatic core and the transformation of sp2 carbon atoms bonded with heteroatoms. In particular, signals around 180 ppm were not observed in the DEPT studies despite to 13C NMR spectrum, implying that there were quaternary carbons and then the terminal CC bonds were oxidized into carbonyl functional groups. The last basis was also observed in the 1H NMR, FTIR and XPS studies.

Matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionization time-of-flight mass spectrometry (MALDI TOF MS) analysis with 2,5-dihydroxybenzoic acid (DHB) matrix showed the continuous signal in mass to charge (m/z) up to few thousands, indicating complex mixture of reaction products. The observed average m/z of the products decreases with reaction time until reaching the stabilization at 5 months (Fig. S19–20). To elucidate the fluorophores structures, subatmospheric (subAP) MALDI coupled to ultra-high mass resolution (HR)MS with orbital trap with DHB matrix was employed for determination of precise m/z of the products up to 1000 Da and estimation of molecular formulas. SubAP MALDI HR mass spectra show many signals assigned to H+ or Na+ adducts of CaHbOcNd; the most abundant species were found in low m/z range 180–300. Despite the large complexity of the spectra, some of the observed signals can be attributed to direct coupling of EDA (C2H8N2) with glucose (C6H12O6); for example, intense peak at m/z 227.1003 was assigned to C8H16O4N2Na+ (two molecules of H2O were eliminated, see Fig. S21). Similarly, signals at m/z 373.2078 and 397.2555 correspond to coupling of 2 molecules of glucose with 2 and 3 molecules of EDA and loss of 6 and 8 molecules of water, respectively. Based on these results, we hypothesize, that the CDs might be supramolecular clusters (i.e., molecular species) with their individual structures linked together through non-covalent interactions rather than individual large polymer molecules. These hypothesis goes well along the HR-TEM measurements showing structures, such as layers with defined interplanar spacing (see Fig. S2-S6).

Concerning the thermal degradation of the CDs, it was evaluated by TGA analysis under N2 (Fig. S22). The TGA curve for the CDs showed a four-step degradation, which differs from the two-step degradation of the glucose. Moreover, we could clearly observe the tendency of stabilization up to 5 months as it was claimed in all the aforementioned characterization techniques. In detail, the first degradation step started at ∼150 °C and could be assigned to the oxygen functional groups [35]. The following major degradations took place between 200 and 500 °C due to the chemical groups covalently linked as amide groups, derivatives of pyridine and the elimination of the aromatic core [36]. In addition, these analyses suggested that the formation and growth of the CDs increases the thermal stability of the material in comparison with their starting materials.

The absorption and fluorescence emission of the dots along the time was investigated. The changes in the optical properties reflect the structure, they depend on the intrinsic electronic states modified by the size of sp2-domains or doping, and existence of the defective states such as surface states or molecular fluorophores [14, 15]. The UV–Vis absorption spectra of the as-prepared dots showed a clear evolution in time (Fig. 4a). The 1 week sample exhibited a slight absorption onset at about 260 nm, corresponding to the π → π* transition of the aromatic sp2 domains, and a strong peak at 325 nm attributed to the n → π* transition of CO, CN bonds, which resembles the absorbance of a fluorophore-like, suggesting the absorption of a specific molecule, as it was already observed [37]. The same absorption band was in the 1 month sample and, additionally, a peak appeared at 278 nm. Finally, after 5 months, the peak at 278 nm increased and the peak at 325 nm broadened and slightly shifted to higher wavelengths, extending up to 550 nm. At this time, the absorption spectrum was similar to the obtained from the microwave-assisted hydrothermal synthesis for the same reactant mixture, glucose and EDA [25]. The absorption corresponding to the π → π* transition of the aromatic sp2 domains and the absorption by the surface states increased with the reaction time.

The photoluminescence (PL) spectra were recorded using excitation wavelengths ranging from 300 nm to 500 nm with a step of 20 nm (Fig. 4bcd and Fig. S23). The excitation-dependent PL measurements exhibited a complex spectrum of light emission with a strong emission covering from the near-violet (NUV) up to the near-infrared (NIR) spectra range in all cases. Despite the complex PL spectra suggesting multiple sources of emission, there were two distinct regions common for samples up to 1 month (Fig. 4bc). Using λex between 300 and 340 nm, the PL peak positions are nearly independent of the excitation and presented broad bands with two shoulders, which could be possibly ascribed to a combination of molecular fluorophores bounded to the particle surface or embedded in the amorphous matrix (Fig. S2 and Fig. S3) [38, 39]. Additionally, the intensity ratio between the first shoulder around λem = 390 nm with respect to the second one at λem = 462 nm decreased along the time (λex = 300 nm, Fig. 4bc), up to its disappearance leading to one single band for the 5-months samples (Fig. 4d).

On the other hand, one broad band was detected when using λex ≥ 360 nm and the whole range emission was excitation-dependent, showing similar maximum emission at 467 nm (λex = 380 nm) for 1 week and 1 month (Fig. 4bc). From 5 months on, a sole channel was founded in the PL spectra (Fig. 4d), exhibiting the maximum emission at 415 nm (λex = 320 nm). The emission wavelength red-shifted with the increasing the λex, showing the typical excitation dependent emission of CDs (Fig. 4bcd) [23, 28, 29]. As the reaction continued, it allowed for the growth of aromatic domains including carbon and/or nitrogen atoms. This agrees well with the absorption and the chemical composition analysis.

The maximum PL emission intensity and its wavelength λem as the functions of λex showed an expected stabilization for 5 months consistent with the aforementioned results (Fig. S24–25). The emission intensity was increasing up to 5 months, while the maximum λem peak position exhibited a blue-shift in the same period. For 1 month, we observed two emission channels, one nearly independent of the excitation and the other displaying excitation-wavelength dependency (Fig. S24a). On the contrary, one single emission channel was noticed from 5 months, which showed excitation-wavelength dependent (i.e., 5 months – 12 months, Fig. S24a). Overall, the emission spectra shift to longer wavelengths along with drastic decrease in fluorescence intensity as we move to higher excitation wavelengths.

Finally, the photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) was evaluated by the absolute method [40]. The PLQYs were measured at the λex that represented the maximum intensity. It was observed that the values were increasing linearly over reaction time up to 5 months, where the values reached the maximum at around 12% (Fig. S26).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Concentration, pH and solvent dependent photoluminescence
ce:section.ce:para: Remarkably, the dots did not only show excitation wavelength dependent emission, but also exhibited concentration, pH and solvent dependent changes in their emission (CDs at 5 months were selected as a model sample). Fig. 5 displayed the λem shifts and the maximum fluorescence intensity with respect to the concentration and pH (further details in SI). Only a few studies have reported the mechanism of concentration-dependent PL properties of carbon-based dots [41, 42]. Our results show that the increment of the dots concentration mostly connected with a blue-shift in the spectra (Fig. 5a and Fig. S27–28). Albeit, using specific low concentrations in the range 0.02–0.05 mg/mL, a red-shift was detected from λem 407 nm to λem 418 nm was detected for the excitation wavelength of λex = 300 nm. Moreover, it caused a considerable increment in the fluorescence intensity as well. Beyond 0.075 mg/mL the intensity was increasing slightly in comparison with the other concentrations, showing a saturation point, which will lead to the inner filter effect [43].

Furthermore, the influence of the pH value on the PL properties of the CDs has also been investigated in the range between 2 and 12 (Fig. 5b and Fig. S29–30). A solution of CDs (pH 7.93) was treated dropwise with dilute HCl or NaOH solution to adjust the pH. If we set our solution (i.e., pH 7.93) as initial point, then increasing the solution's acidity from pH 8–2 lead to an increased in the fluorescence intensity of the CDs (Fig. 5b). On the contrary, a stabilization near to neutral pH followed by a continuous loss of the fluorescence intensity was detected between neutral and basic conditions (pH 8–12). Regarding the maximum λem peak, a red-shift was displayed in both, acidic and basic conditions. In detail, from 424 to 434 nm as the pH value decreased from 8 to 2 and from 424 to 438 nm when it increased from 8 to 12 (i.e., excited at λex = 340 nm). Similar behavior was found in different excitation wavelengths (Fig. S31). This phenomenon is justifiable for the change of surface charge induced by the protonation and deprotonation from the oxygen and nitrogen containing functional groups, such as, hydroxyl, carboxyl and amino groups [44, 45].

We further explored the PL properties when CDs were dissolved in different solvents. CDs showed excellent solubility in water and many organic solvents, particularly polar, protic and aprotic solvents. Compared to polar solvents, the dots hardly dissolved in non-polar (e.g., diethyl ether, chloroform, and ethyl acetate) solvents and precipitated after a few days. Despite the concentration and pH dependent PL were the maximum PL peak was maintained, herein CDs exhibited different maximum PL peaks after dissolving in different solvents (Fig. S32). For instance, in solvents based on OH, the maximum was λex = 340 nm instead of λex = 320 nm in water. From the structural techniques describe above (Fig. 2) we concluded that the dots contained many functional groups, such as -OH, -NH2, -COOH, which can form strong hydrogen bonds with alcohol solvents, leading to a decrease of the fluorescence intensity as well. Overall, the hydrogen bond interactions between the CDs surface and the polar protic solvents make CDs present solvent-dependent PL. Consequently, any alteration of the structure will lead to a modification of the optical properties.

The fluorescence emission of CDs displayed an interesting pH, concentration, and solvent dependence. In this regard, it is essential to notice that the majority of the CDs-based logic gates depend on the changes in the fluorescence response of CDs [46]. Consequently, we showed that by using H+ and OH¯ as inputs and the fluorescence intensities as outputs, the unique fluorescence response of the CDs could be converted to logic gates (further information in SI, Figs. S33-S34).

We propose that the spontaneous formation of the carbon dots starts with an opening chain reaction of the glucose (Fig. 6 ) [47]. Afterwards, the nitrogen of the EDA acted as nucleophilic agent and reacted with the open chain of glucose. The basic solution from EDA was involved in the dehydration of glucose to finally form CC. The same procedure was repeated, and carbon atoms interact consecutively with EDA until they reach the complete fluorophore structure formation of CDs. It could be also noticed that the formation of the CDs come along with color change of the solution from colorless to yellow (Fig. 1). The possible structures were constructed using the molecular editor Avogadro [48], and subsequently optimized by a built-in molecular mechanics routine using a Universal Force Field (UFF). The obtained molecular geometries were subsequently optimized using Density Functional Theory (DFT) as implemented in Vienna Ab initio Simulation Package (VASP) (Fig. 6, further details SI) [49, 50]. The presented structures were elucidated from the basis of the aromatic core formation and further characterization techniques that were discussed above. We note that the proposed reaction chain cannot exclude other structures as well. Overall, the energetics profiles were found to be favorable for the formation of the possible structures which formed the CDs-like structure.

The summary formulas of the two last reaction products shown in the Fig. 6b are C18H26O2N6 and C20H30N8O2. Corresponding m/z values of the protonated pseudomolecular peaks are 359.2190 and 415.2564, respectively. Though these signals were not dominant in the spectra, we observed time trend where the ratio of the signals of C20H30N8O2/C18H26O2N6 increases in time indicating reaction progress (see Fig. S20). The ratio of these two products in 5 months and 12 months was almost the same indicating reaction stabilization in good agreement with MALDI TOF MS (Fig. S19). Complexity of the MALDI mass spectra and many other observed products indicate different, yet similar, possible reaction pathways. We need to note, that the plethora of different signals might originate also from interaction with MALDI matrix and in-source fragmentation process, as the CDs significantly absorb at the wavelength of the employed desorption laser (i.e., 355 nm, see Fig. 4a).

Generally, the differences obtained in this spontaneous formation in contrast with microwave reaction are mostly related to chemical structure and chemical composition. In detail, the nitrogen content obtained by XPS (N/C = 0.21, Table S1) was lower than the dots obtained via microwave approach [25]. Thus, increasing the energy of the reaction lead us to think that a more efficient doping and a more detailed structure composition could be obtained. Nevertheless, if we agree with Rigodanza and co-workers that elevated temperature is needed for the carbonization step and therefore, obtaining the genuine carbon dots [22], we present aggregates of polycyclic aromatic molecules (i.e., molecular fluorophore species), rather than carbon nanoparticles in this work. However, the morphological, chemical and optical properties found it here for the as-prepared molecular fluorophore species were already ascribed to certain carbon related dots [51, 52]. For instance, pure molecular samples could display d-spacings of 0.21 nm observed by HRTEM [53]. Therefore, even when particles are observed by HRTEM, the morphological results and crystal structures observed do not rule out the presence of molecular species instead of carbon nanoparticles. Then, are we sure about the possible idea of carbon dots as fluorescence carbon nanoparticles or most probably a combination of molecular species that looks like nanoparticles?

In this work, using traditional techniques, we have demonstrated that the formation of carbon dots is ascribed to molecular species. In detail, we elucidated the structure of carbon dots along the time at room temperature, which allowed us to develop a model system to mimic their optical properties toward a unique carbon dots design. The chemical structure characterization was carried out by a broad set of methods, indicating the formation of a compact network of molecular species. Density functional theory calculations presented here provided evidence supporting the proposed formation-chain. Finally, our extensive findings related to the PL emission under different concentrations, pHs and solvents pave the way to exploit these CDs logic gate fluorescent sensors. We hope that the results presented here will help to avoid inconsistencies and errors in future works focusing on the design of carbon dots.
The manuscript was written through contributions of all authors. All authors have given approval to the final version of the manuscript.

I. Jénnifer Gómez: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Manuel Vázquez Sulleiro: Investigation, Methodology, Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Naděžda Pizúrová: Investigation, Methodology, Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Antonín Bednařík: Investigation, Methodology, Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Petr Lepcio: Investigation, Methodology, Data curation, Writing – review & editing. David Holec: Investigation, Methodology, Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Jan Preisler: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Lenka Zajíčková: Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

The authors are grateful to Prof. Emilio Pérez, Prof. Petr Klán, and Prof. Vladimír Šindelář for allowing to use of the TGA, UV–Vis and fluorescence spectrometer, and the MW reactor. Financial resources related to these equipment were RECETOX research infrastructure (supported by MEYS CR under LM2018121 and 02.1.01/0.0/0.0/18_046/0015975) and the CETOCOEN EXCELLENCE Teaming 2 project Horizon2020 (857560). The computational results presented have been achieved [in part] using the Vienna Scientific Cluster (VSC).

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: V. Michaud, J. Pracht, F. Schilfarth, C. Damm, B. Platzer, P. Haines, C. Harreiß, D.M. Guldi, E. Spiecker, W. Peukert, Well-separated water-soluble carbon dots: via gradient chromatography, Nanoscale. 13 (2021) 13116–13128. doi: 10.1039/d1nr02562g.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Formation mechanism of carbon dots: from chemical structures to fluorescent behaviors

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Cao, M. Zan, F. Chen, X. Kou, Y. Liu, P. Wang, Q. Mei, Z. Hou, W.-F. Dong, L. Li, Formation mechanism of carbon dots: from chemical structures to fluorescent behaviors, Carbon N Y. 194 (2022). doi: 10.1016/j.carbon.2022.03.058.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Small molecular organic nanocrystals resemble carbon nanodots in terms of their properties

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Khan, A. Sharma, S. Ghoshal, S. Jain, M.K. Hazra, C.K. Nandi, Small molecular organic nanocrystals resemble carbon nanodots in terms of their properties, Chem. Sci. 9 (2017) 175–180. doi: 10.1039/c7sc02528a.
